items.rationale: Seen as the next foundational primitive on par with smart contracts — unlocks scaling (rollups), private DeFi, identity, and AI verification. Backed with capital (Aztec, Aleo, zkSync) and dedicated research.

citations.quote: i still think people underestimate how transformational zero knowledge proofs are
the implications of zero knowledge proofs being a thing may be as dramatic as the implications of smart contracts
Encryption & privacy will be essential for blockchains to broaden their utility and gain widespread adoption

items.rationale: Post-FTX, the house's central thesis: trustless on-chain finance is structurally safer than CeFi and is the policy answer to the 2022 collapses. Anchors the 'regulate apps, not protocols' worldview.

citations.quote: Do the collapses of FTX, Celsius, and Terra signal the end of crypto? No, but they do show why we need #DeFi
DeFi is better policy than CeFi
We believe on-chain lending is an essential DeFi primitive

items.rationale: Viewed as web3's native coordination form, but the excitement is now coupled with urgency about legal legitimacy (UNAs, California DAO bill) and better governance design (indirect/liquid democracy) rather than naive direct democracy.
items.age_note: broad enthusiasm 2021-2022; by 2023 reframed toward legal-wrapper pragmatism and governance realism

citations.quote: DAOs are a groundbreaking way for people with shared interests to collaborate towards common goals
DAOs continue to be threatened by a number of significant legal challenges... This new California DAO bill could change all of that

items.rationale: Player-owned assets and fully on-chain game logic seen as a major unlock; backed via Halliday, CCP/EVE Online. Treated as a key consumer on-ramp.

citations.quote: I believe fully on-chain games will fill a similar niche in coming years
We believe CCPGames will help shape the next generation of web3 games

items.rationale: Fast-rising 2023 theme — crypto as the ownership/coordination/incentive layer for AI ('how machines can own'), collective AI, verifiable models. Explicitly 'early innings.'
items.age_note: emergent 2023 theme (seed: kosenlabs 2021); not yet a deployment thesis

citations.quote: AI = how machines can think; crypto = how machines can own; AI + crypto = how machines can think on their own
We're in the early innings of some fascinating interplay between crypto+AI/ML

items.rationale: Token-incentivized real-world networks (telecom, knowledge graphs, storage) that undercut incumbents. Crystallized into a clean investment test in 2023.

citations.quote: Helium's use of crypto incentives spurred the world's fastest-growing wireless network
The essential question when considering building a DePIN network: Verification cost < incumbent margin

items.rationale: Rollups are live production infrastructure; the future is explicitly accepted as multi-chain (LayerZero). Diversity of execution environments seen as a feature.

citations.quote: rollups like are actually live today with production code, which is kind of amazing
There is no longer any question that the future of crypto and web3 is multi-chain

items.rationale: Web3's dramatically lower take rates (OpenSea 2.5% vs web2's 30-100%) seen as a durable structural edge, with music as the worst-broken web2 category to disrupt.

citations.quote: Sound is music without the middleman—a music platform and suite of web3-native tools that empower artists
People always talk about web3's lower take rates, and the impact this could have on the creator economy

items.rationale: Still core to the thesis (wave mints, cc0 licensing, Can't-Be-Evil licenses), but the framing has matured from speculative scarcity to community/utility and Pokémon-card-like sustainable collectibles.
items.age_note: peak hype 2021-early 2022; by late 2022 explicitly reframed away from ultra-scarcity speculation

citations.quote: the long run digital collectibles business model will look more like Pokemon cards than the ultra-scarcity pervasive in NFTs today
An NFT mint largely determines who your community is

items.rationale: EigenLayer-style pooling of staked security to bootstrap new middleware/services without standing up fragmented trust networks; convergence of Ethereum and Cosmos approaches.
items.age_note: emerging 2022-08 to 2023-02; interest signaled, not yet a stated firm-wide conviction

citations.quote: Restaking and mesh security also seem to overlap. Interesting to see Cosmos and Ethereum pushing in the same directions

items.rationale: The locus of 2022's catastrophes (FTX, Celsius, Voyager, BlockFi). Trust-based intermediaries seen as the real risk; custody itself framed as a liability that must be regulated unlike trustless systems.

citations.quote: Shoutout to the CeFi players out there demonstrating unequivocally why trust inherently needs to be regulated differently than trustless
custody is a liability

items.rationale: Treated as the single biggest economic drag on the industry — four years without guidance, enforcement deemed unscalable and deliberately uncertainty-fostering, driving builders offshore. The dominant 2023 anxiety.

citations.quote: Enforcement isn't scalable... it's meant to create the illusion that GaryGensler is being proactive
It has now been FOUR years since the last crypto-related guidance from the SEC
regulatory uncertainty is already the biggest economic drag on the sector

items.rationale: Root cause of Terra/UST collapse identified as bad/endogenous collateral, not algorithms per se. The riskiest design; uncollateralized variants dismissed outright.

citations.quote: Uncollateralized stablecoins are basically just meme coins with no potential upside
bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra, not 'algorithmic stablecoins'

items.rationale: Deployed proofs run at ~80 bits flagged as a live, exploitable systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem securing hundreds of millions; prioritizing performance over security could break trust.

citations.quote: if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology
Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible. This is not an academic question

items.rationale: Repeatedly warned against — public token sales to US persons and the 'utility token' dodge seen as legally dead and a magnet for bad actors; decentralization is the only safe path.
items.age_note: stance hardened over time; by 2022-2023 treated as settled (the SEC 'won' on ICOs)

citations.quote: Don't sell tokens to U.S. persons for fundraising purposes. There is no issue that the SEC has been more clear or consistent about
Result of the LBRY case should be the final nail in the coffin of the 'utility token' approach

items.rationale: Direct token-voting democracy seen as unworkable at scale — low turnout enables capture by small motivated groups and governance attacks; needs delegation, oversight, a 'free press' layer.

citations.quote: you simply can't govern through direct democracy at any kind of meaningful scale
people's expectations of what most DAOs are capable of needs to be significantly adjusted downwards

items.rationale: Smart-contract development described as costly, non-agile and 'far from secure'; bridges and exchanges keep getting hacked because security rests on fragile human processes.

citations.quote: smart contract development is the opposite from agile... it's very costly... far from easy and far from secure
exchanges just keep getting hacked... humans don't scale

items.rationale: Post-FTX 'proof of reserves' from centralized exchanges (e.g. Binance) judged superficial and exploitable — doesn't address negative balances or address control.
items.age_note: specific to the Nov 2022 post-FTX moment

citations.quote: Binance's system is useless and easily exploited by them

items.rationale: Treated as the clear category leader by developer mindshare, fees, and economic security; post-Merge superior; secures a ~$400B economy; consistently argued NOT to be a security (decentralized, no managerial efforts).

citations.quote: Ethereum is the clear leader. Its overwhelming mindshare among web3 devs
On Ethereum, it's used to secure a $400 billion economy of ETH, ERC20 tokens, and NFTs

items.rationale: Regarded as a technically superior, cryptographically scarce 'better gold'; robust, never-hacked base chain; clearly not a security. Respected but not the innovation frontier.

citations.quote: from a purely technical point of view Bitcoin is just evidently a better version of gold than gold is
blockchains like Bitcoin and Ethereum have never been hacked

items.rationale: Held up as the success story that survived 2022 volatility — overcollateralized/exogenous-backed decentralized stablecoins 'performed superbly' and are a capital-efficiency unlock; contrasted favorably with Terra.

citations.quote: The vast majority of decentralized stablecoins have performed superbly. The riskiest are under-collateralized & rely on 'endogenous' collateral
Plenty of examples that have successfully navigated all the recent volatility... a huge unlock for capital efficiency

items.rationale: The canonical cautionary tale — extremely undercollateralized with endogenous collateral; its collapse is repeatedly cited as the root-cause example of bad stablecoin design.

citations.quote: bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra
Terra being extremely undercollateralized

items.rationale: Cited as a publicly visible warning the SEC ignored; widely viewed with skepticism in the legal community well before the collapse — an emblem of CeFi/exchange-token risk.
items.age_note: acute around the Nov 2022 FTX collapse

citations.quote: FTT was widely viewed with skepticism in the legal community, which has consistently been advising against FTX's approach in the US

items.rationale: Centered in the SEC securities fight; the LBRY outcome is read as bad-portending for Ripple. Notably, the team thinks the case's broader significance is overstated.
items.age_note: team views the litigation outcome as largely moot for the industry

citations.quote: the LBRY result doesn't portend a good result for Ripple
does the outcome of SEC v. Ripple actually matter?

items.rationale: Dismissed as lacking fundamental economic basis — a meme without a reason to thrive.
items.age_note: stated once 2021-11, never restated; carries forward as the firm's meme-coin skepticism

citations.quote: its provenance as a meme leaves it without much of a fundamental economic reason to thrive

risk_regime.rationale: Despite a bear market, the FTX aftermath, and open hostilities with the SEC, the house posture is structurally bullish and actively deploying (Crypto Fund 4; leading rounds into 2023). The downturn is explicitly framed as a healthy 'good faith innovation' cleansing that favors decentralized, compliant builders. Tactically sober/cautious near-term (regulatory drag, security risks), but conviction in the long-run trajectory ('still early', ~1995-internet analogy) is undiminished — advice to founders is 'Stay The Course.'

notes: Recency-weighted as instructed: the 2023 corpus is dominated by (1) an intensifying regulatory war with the SEC ('regulate apps, not protocols') and (2) a post-FTX flight to decentralization/DeFi as the safety thesis — these are the loudest current signals. Earlier (2021-mid-2022) NFT and metaverse euphoria has visibly cooled and matured rather than reversed: the team still believes in NFTs but reframed them from speculative scarcity toward community/utility and Pokémon-like sustainable collectibles (2022-11), so those entries are marked structural-but-cooled, not abandoned. Crypto×AI is a genuinely new 2023 theme, flagged 'early innings' (extrapolated/grounded, not yet a capital thesis). The corpus is clearly multi-voice: a policy/legal voice (regulation, DAOs, securities law), a market-design/economist voice (auctions, royalties, governance, vampire attacks), a cryptography/research voice (ZK, PoS security, SNARK bit-security, consensus), and an infra/investing voice (fund announcements, L1/L2, DePIN) — 'consensus' here means recurring, cross-voice agreement rather than unanimity. Conviction scores are intensity of enthusiasm/concern, not probabilities. Notable internal tension: the firm champions decentralization as a regulatory shield while simultaneously insisting decentralization is 'not a ticket to lawlessness' — pragmatic compliance is the through-line. No specific small-cap token picks surface beyond ETH/BTC and the decentralized-stablecoin category; the house expresses sector/primitive conviction far more than token conviction.
